content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

May 30, 2019

Christine Davi
City Attorney
512 Pierce St.
Monterey, CA 93940

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-082

Dear Ms. Davi:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Monterey's
Neighborhood Improvement Program Committee Member William Tipton regarding the conflict of
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). ¹ Please note that we are only providing
advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of
interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are
not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice
we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

1. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Committee Member Tipton from
taking part in decisions concerning four proposed projects located within 500 feet of his residence?

2. If Committee Member Tipton is disqualified from one or more proposed projects located
within 500 feet of his residence, may he take part in decisions on other proposed projects in which
he does not have a conflict of interest?

CONCLUSIONS

1. The financial effect of each of the four governmental decisions on the Committee
Member's residence is foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and presumed material under
Regulation 18702.2 (a)(7). However, three of the four projects will likely have only a nominal,
inconsequential or insignificant financial effect on his residence, so he is not disqualified from those
three decisions.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-082
Page No. 2

2. Although Committee Member Tipton is disqualified from taking part in decisions on one of the four proposed projects, he may take part in decisions related to the remaining projects so long as a decision on the project in which he is disqualified is considered first and a final decision is reached by the Committee without his participation.

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Neighborhood Improvement Program ("Program")

The Program is set forth in Monterey Charter section 6.6. It was established in 1988, and its purpose is to ensure "... that a minimum portion of the City's annual budget is expended to improve the residential neighborhoods of the City and to provide for capital projects of community-wide benefit." Charter section 6.6 requires that the City Council "... appropriate at least 16% of the Transient Occupancy Tax ("TOT") estimated to be collected during the fiscal year to be expended on Neighborhood and Community Improvements." The Program's budget this year exceeds $3 million.

The City has 16 neighborhoods, and the City Council appoints one resident from each neighborhood to serve on the Program Committee ("Committee"). The City Council also appoints alternates to serve on the Committee. Most, but not all, neighborhoods have an alternate representative. Anyone may nominate a project for the Committee's consideration. The Committee "... recommends [to the City Council] a list of capital improvements to be accomplished in each neighborhood." (Charter section 6.6(d).) From the recommendations of the Committee, the City Council includes the Program in the annual budget. The City Council approves the projects, priorities, and expenditures. (Charter section 6.6(e).)

The City Council appointed Committee Member Tipton to serve as the Committee's representative for the Downtown neighborhood. Committee Member Tipton has a real property interest in his residence of $2,000 or more, and this residence is located within 500 feet of the four projects described below.

Downtown Neighborhood Proposed Projects

Project DT-3: Trash Receptacle

If approved, this project would install a trash can within a wooden frame at the corner of East Franklin Street and Figueroa Street near Jack's Ball Park, a 3.7-acre multi-use municipal sport park. This park includes a ball field, bleachers, and night lighting; a tot play area; and public restrooms. The estimated cost of this project is $5,100. The project is intended to make it more convenient for people at the Sports Center (located across the street on East Franklin) and Jack's Ball Park to throw away trash. The project nomination form describes the need for the project as follows:

This pocket park and the adjacent sidewalks are frequently littered with trash. Figueroa and Franklin are very popular parking areas for visitors, Sports Center users, and transients. Trash in gutters and on sidewalk borders is very common. A trash receptacle at the corner would make it

File No. A-19-082
Page No. 3

convenient for residents and visitors to dispose of trash outright or
retrieved [sic] from the ground while passing by.

The project area is not visible from Committee Member Tipton's residence. A new trash
receptacle at this location may increase the cleanliness of the area by eliminating trash and visual
blight.

Project DT-4: Dog Waste Bag Dispenser Station

This project would install a dog waste bag dispenser station on Pearl Street at Figueroa
Street, and replace three concrete panels of sidewalk and install an American with Disabilities Act
compliant concrete pad. This project is adjacent to the Monterey Tennis Center, which is
immediately next to Jack's Ball Park, described above in DT-3. The Tennis Center is open daily
from 9 a.m. - 10 p.m., and it has six lighted tennis courts and a pro shop. The estimated cost of this
project is $7,500.

The project nomination form describes the need for the project by stating "[t]he lawn and
landscaped area along the tennis courts is very popular with local, itinerant, and visiting pet owners.
It is also popular with students and tennis watchers who often find the grass compromised by pet
droppings that have not been disposed of."

The project area is visible from the sidewalk abutting Committee Member Tipton's
residence. The elimination of pet waste from the vicinity would increase the cleanliness of the area.

Project DT-10: Curb Ramp Improvements

This project would reform the curb ramps and travel distances at the intersections of
Figueroa and Cortes along Pearl Street. The estimated cost of this project is $325,000, and the
purpose is to improve pedestrian safety and install new curb ramps. There is a large volume of
pedestrian traffic due to a youth transitional housing shelter located on Pearl Street, Jack's Ball
Park, the Tennis Center, the Sports Center, and a nearby school.

The project nomination form describes the need for the project by stating "[t]he curbs at the
intersections of Cortes and Pearl and at Figueroa and Pearl are not ADA accessible. By adding bulb
outs into the streets, the path of travel would be reduced for pedestrians and the length of the ramp
would be increased. Both of these intersections serve as heavily used walking corridors for residents
and visitors of all ages."

There have been no pedestrian versus motor vehicle accidents at this location and the
existing concrete curbs are in good condition. The project will reduce the exposure to a pedestrian
crossing the street by 12 feet, thereby improving pedestrian safety. It also improves the path of
travel for disabled persons by installing accessible curb ramps where they do not presently exist at
the South side of the intersection. Committee Member Tipton's residence is not the corner near a
project intersection, but one of the intersections is visible when standing out front of Committee
Member Tipton's residence, which is toward the middle of Pearl Street.

File No. A-19-082
Page No. 4


Project CW-46: Artificial Turf Field

This project would install artificial turf on the infield and new sod in the outfield at Jack's
Ball Park, described above in Project DT-3. The estimated cost of this project is $550,000. The
purpose of this project is to reduce required maintenance for the park.

The project nomination form describes the need for the project by stating the "Project would
increase play ability [sic] and safety of players. Installing artificial turf on the infield is likely to
increase the intensity of use as it will allow more baseball games to be played and more baseball
practices to take place because the artificial turf will eliminate the need for maintenance and
preparation in between games or practices." The project could increase parking in the neighborhood
on the weekends, however, there is a City parking garage located a few blocks away. Replacing the
sod may slightly improve the appearance of the existing grass outfield.

Base Allocation Voting

The Committee votes on which of the approximately 100 proposed projects to recommend
to City Council. The number of projects the Committee ultimately recommends to the City Council
depends on the amount of available Program budget, and the cost of each project. The top projects
funded within the Program's budget, usually 40 to 45 of them, are recommended to the City
Council. Committee Member Tipton's neighborhood has 4 or 5 proposed projects.

Each neighborhood is assigned a base allocation of $7.50 per person residing in that
neighborhood. Therefore, each neighborhood has a different amount of base allocation to commit to
projects. The base allocation for the neighborhoods ranges from $727 to $38,182, and it may be
accumulated for up to two years. Committee Member Tipton's neighborhood currently has
approximately $1,800 in base allocation. During the Committee's meeting at which project voting
occurs, each project's scope and cost estimate are described to the Committee. After receiving
public comment, staff asks each Committee Member to state which project(s) he/she would like to
apply the neighborhood's allotment of base allocation. Committee Members also have the option to
apply none or only part of their base allocations and carryover the remainder to the next Program
year.

ANALYSIS

Interests

Under Section 87100, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local government shall
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." A
public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on the official, a Member of his or her immediate family, or on an
enumerated interest. (Section 87103.) Such interests include "[a]ny real property in which the

File No. A-19-082
Page No. 5

public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more."
(Section 87103(b).) Here, Committee Member Tipton has an economic interest in his downtown
residence that is worth more than $2,000.²

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official
or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701(a).) Regarding financial interests not explicitly involved
in a decision, a financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In
general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical
or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Regulation 18702.2 provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on real property in which an official has a financial interest is material under
certain enumerated circumstances. The regulation provides that the effect is material whenever the
governmental decision "[i]nvolves property located 500 feet or less from the property line of the
parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any measurable
impact on the official's property ...." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(7).)

Here, the financial effect of each of the four governmental decisions on Mr. Tipton's
residence is foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and presumed material under Regulation
18702.2(a)(7) unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the four governmental decisions at
issue would not have any measurable impact on the residence owned by Mr. Tipton. In addition,
Regulation 18702(b) provides that "the financial effect of a governmental decision is not material if
it is nominal, inconsequential, or insignificant." As explained in detail above, Project DT-3 involves
placing a trash can at the corner of East Franklin Street and Figueroa Street near Jack's Ball Park
that won't be visible from Committee Member Tipton's residence; Project DT-4 involves the
installation of a dog waste bag dispenser station on Pearl Street at Figueroa Street that may be
visible from the sidewalk adjacent to his residence; and Project DT-10 proposes the modification of
curbs, which are currently in good condition, at two intersections, one of which is visible from the
front of his residence located toward the middle of Pearl Street.

Although each of the three proposed projects may provide some minor positive impacts to
the surrounding area, it does not appear the financial effect of any of the three projects on
Committee Member Tipton's real property will likely be more than nominal, inconsequential, or
insignificant. Therefore, Committee Member Tipton is not disqualified from those three decisions.

However the same is not true with Project CW-46, which proposes the installation of
artificial turf on the infield and new sod in the outfield at Jack's Ball Park, at an estimated cost of
$550,000. This project intends to reduce maintenance for the park, allowing for more baseball

² Committee Member Tipton is a public official for purposes of the Act. (See Connors Advice Letter, No. I-91-
206 [Committee Members are public officials under the Act since "the city council may only expend money on projects
'recommended' by the committee, the committee is making final governmental decisions or preventing governmental
decisions when it chooses not to recommend a project"].)

File No. A-19-082
Page No. 6

games and baseball practices, which will increase the intensity of the park's use and parking
demand in the neighborhood on the weekends. In addition, replacing the sod will likely improve the
appearance of the outfield. As compared to the other three projects, Project CW-46 is larger in scale
and will likely have a greater impact on the surrounding areas.

Accordingly, Committee Member Tipton is disqualified from taking part in decisions related
to Project CW-46. (See Regulation 18707.)

Segmentation

The final issue to be determined is whether the decisions relating to the approximately 100
proposed projects may be segmented pursuant to Regulation 18706. In some cases, large, complex
decisions may be segmented into separate decisions so that even if an official has a disqualifying
interest in one component of the series of decisions, that official may still participate in the other
components in which he does not have a disqualifying interest. (Borger Advice Letter, No. 1-15-
110; and Whitman Advice Letter, No. A-14-186.) However, where a decision in which an official
has a disqualifying interest is "inextricably interrelated" to the other decisions that comprise the
series of decisions, the official is disqualified from taking part in all of those decisions. (Mattas
Advice Letter, No. A-16-113.)

Specifically, Regulation 18706 permits an agency to segment certain decisions and provides
in pertinent part:

(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be
broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated
to the decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial
interest;³

(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
segmented from the other decisions;

(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is
considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the
disqualified official's participation in any way;⁴ and

(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has
been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result
in a reopening of, or otherwise financially affect, the decision from
which the official was disqualified.

³ For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when the result of one decision will
effectively determine, affirm, modify, or alter the result of another decision. (Regulation 18706(b).)

⁴ We note that you have detailed the general procedure in which the Committee plans to make project
selections in your request for assistance. However, unless there has been a final decision reached on Project CW-46
prior to Council Member Tipton's involvement in other decision, the procedure you proposed will not satisfy the
segmentation requirements under the Act.

File No. A-19-082
Page No. 7

Based upon the facts provided, the ultimate selection of the projects will be limited by the
available funding. In light of the fact that Project CW-46 will require a large portion of the available
funds, Committee Member Tipton may take part in decisions regarding other projects only to the
extent that the decisions are properly segmented. Consequently, any procedure used must ensure
that a decision on Project CW-46 is considered first and a final decision is reached by the
Committee without Committee Member Tipton's participation in any way. In other words, he may
only participate in the consideration of the other projects in which he is not disqualified after a final
decision on Project CW-46, in which he has a conflict of interest, has been made.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Jack Woodside
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

JW:aja

sections.question:
1. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Committee Member Tipton from 
taking part in decisions concerning four proposed projects located within 500 feet of his residence? 
2. If Committee Member Tipton is disqualified from one or more proposed projects located 
within 500 feet of his residence, may he take part in decisions on other proposed projects in which 
he does not have a conflict of interest?
sections.conclusion:
1. The financial effect of each of the four governmental decisions on the Committee 
Member's residence is foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and presumed material under 
Regulation 18702.2 (a)(7). However, three of the four projects will likely have only a nominal, 
inconsequential or insignificant financial effect on his residence, so he is not disqualified from those 
three decisions.2. Although Committee Member Tipton is disqualified from taking part in decisions on one 
of the four proposed projects, he may take part in decisions related to the remaining projects so long 
as a decision on the project in which he is disqualified is considered first and a final decision is 
reached by the Committee without his participation.

games and baseball practices, which will increase the intensity of the park's use and parking

Committee without Committee Member Tipton's participation in any way. In other words, he may 
only participate in the consideration of the other projects in which he is not disqualified after a final 
decision on Project CW-46, in which he has a conflict of interest, has been made. 
JW:aja

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Committee Member Tipton from 
taking part in decisions concerning four proposed projects located within 500 feet of his residence? 
2. If Committee Member Tipton is disqualified from one or more proposed projects located 
within 500 feet of his residence, may he take part in decisions on other proposed projects in which 
he does not have a conflict of interest?

CONCLUSION: 1. The financial effect of each of the four governmental decisions on the Committee 
Member's residence is foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and presumed material under 
Regulation 18702.2 (a)(7). However, three of the four projects will likely have only a nominal, 
inconsequential or insignificant financial effect on his residence, so he is not disqualified from those 
three decisions.2. Although Committee Member Tipton is disqualified from taking part in decisions on one 
of the four proposed projects, he may take part in decisions related to the remaining projects so long 
as a decision on the project in which he is disqualified is considered first and a final decision is 
reached by the Committee without his participation.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 May 30, 2019 Christine Davi City Attorney 512 Pierce St. Monterey, CA 93940 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-19-082 Dear Ms. Davi: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Monterey's Neighborhood Improvement Program Committee Member William Tipton regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). ¹ Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTIONS 1. Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit Committee Member Tipton from taking part in decisions concerning four proposed projects located within 500 feet of his residence? 2. If Committee Member Tipton is disqualified from one or more proposed projects located within 500 feet of his residence, may he take part in decisions on other proposed projects in which he does not have a conflict of interest? CONCLUSIONS 1. The financial effect of each of the four governmental decisions on the Committee Member's residence is foreseeable under Regulation 18701(a) and presumed material under Regulation 18702.2 (a)(7). However, three of the four projects will likely have only a nominal, inconsequential or insignificant financial effect on his residence, so he is not disqualified from those three decisions. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-19-082 Page No. 2 2. Although Committee Member Tipton is disqualified from taking part in decisions on one of the four proposed projects, he may take part in decisions related to the remaining projects so long as a decision on the project in which he is disqualified is considered first and a final decision is reached by the Committee without his participation. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER Neighborhood Improvement Program ("Program") The Program is set forth in Monterey Charter section 6.6. It was established in 1988, and its purpose is to ensure "... that a minimum portion of the City's annual budget is expended to improve the residential neighborhoods of the City and to provide for capital projects of community-wide benefit."
